text: nature, destabilizing. Neuroreactive compounds, whether imaginal or material, tear apart a person’s set patterns. When national intelligence agencies poured funding into psychedelic research hoping for the key to mind control, they found instead that the deeper your dive, the more control factors break down. The journey inward is equally as abyssal as imagining the fantastic voids at the very edge of celestial space. As an initiatory element, this destabilization is useful in ritual contexts where new elements are introduced to replace what has been unmoored, where symbolic and allegorical patterns create new pathways and relationships, but without this ritual setting the results are not as predictable. When the symbolic and allegorical patterns in the environment have been manipulated or presented to reflect dubious motives, such as an environment whose aesthetics are determined by marketing firms and corporations, the results can be terrifying or even psychosomatically damaging. Anomalous phenomena and what are commonly known as paranormal encounters demonstrate similar characteristics of destabilization. Confrontations with situations that violate our routine programming literally crack the imaginal structure that keeps us moored and secure to commercial reality. AS I OPENED THE DOOR As I opened the door, Alex (Tanous) smiled and extended his hand. Suddenly I froze, remembering that this psychic was known for something else—an uncanny ability to foretell death. He once told me he got his most accurate death impressions when he shook somebody’s hand. Hey! I thought, I don’t want to shake this guy’s hand. Instead I threw my arms around him, and we embraced. And as we embraced, I thought to myself in rapid succession—“I don’t want to believe Alex can do any of these things!” And then, “I don’t believe he can! —Michael Grosso, “Nine Reasons to Fear the Paranormal” 4 Jacques Vallee and other scientists taking a reasoned approach to studying the unexplained have shown that experiences with the unknown are often traumatizing and life changing. To move from the daylight world of objective experience into the twilight world of the unexplained can be deeply disturbing. It leaves one without an objective measure for reality, puts one outside the boundaries of what is easily explainable, and can make one an object of ridicule and rejection. The original methodology of the Society for Psychical Research used the tools of anthropology and philosophy more often than it did those of psychology or the hard sciences. The journals from the late nineteenth century are comprised mostly of recounted tales, second-hand information, metaphysical speculation, and book reviews. What hard science there is amounts to photographic evidence and specialists in the art of illusion and sleight of hand debunking or affirming the legitimacy of mediums. The story always remained at the forefront of their investigation, because the story was the motivating factor to the phenomena. The professional illusionist is perceived as an essential tool of the skeptic, yet in the same way that we miss something by calling the element of individuation in ethnography “bias,” when the illusionist is not given full breath to do more than bust hoaxes, we lose a valuable means of deepening our relationship with reality. What does it mean that even the most debased televangelists can be credited as a vehicle of liberation for some of those they pretend to heal? UNANTICIPATED SIDE EFFECTS George Hansen, 5 a former associate at the Rhine Research Center in Durham, North Carolina, has written extensively on the consistent struggle in the world of professional parapsychology to maintain credibility in the face of phenomenal experience. Unanticipated side effects attend the research when results are achieved or seem close to being achieved; things start to happen that destabilize the experiment and the experimenters acting as observers. Divorces, job losses, suicides, lack of funding, or blatant frauds end up ruining years of inquiry. It’s one thing to study traditional or religious groups that have some element of the paranormal in their practice; it’s a totally different thing to study it within the context of everyday life, where there are no bounds to the experience and no set structure for how it should be interpreted. The fringe nature of paranormal phenomena plays a part in this to some extent. Many of the people drawn to studying them or who are themselves being studied are already on the fringes of society. Having stepped outside of the norm, they are ripe for destabilizing events and not always ready for their consequence. Those who begin to study these phenomena with solid beliefs already in place are either shaken to the point that they become “true believers,” dropping all scientific credibility and distancing themselves from what is considered legitimate research, or they become rigid debunkers to protect their own sanity and credibility. This is a common element in any situation in which a crisis in belief leads to a breakdown of binary thinking. William Sargant’s work on possession and trance states shows how extreme emotional states can lead to transpersonal experiences. In many studies nothing happens at all, or the results are not repeatable, making it impossible to gather a baseline of data for reasonable comparison. Even in cases where laboratory tests seem to indicate positive effects, at one point or another some bit of trickery or lax research controls seem to emerge in place of legitimate ability. In traditional cultures the use of sleight of hand and showmanship is well known as part of the shamanic repertoire. To the Western mind this appears as fraudulent; traditional healers, however, will explain the necessity of such tricks and techniques to distract and engage the spirits. Even when sleight of hand is apparent in anomalous phenomena, the interplay of events often shows that there is something more going on, but to justify this in the laboratory, under strict scientific methodology, is impossible. SALIENT SLEIGHT OF HAND One of the most common means used to debunk claims of the paranormal is a thorough knowledge of the art of illusion, stage magic, and the psychologically potent tricks of mentalism.
